speaker: interviewer
text: June 7, 1995. The survivor is Cantor Morris Lang. This is reel 3. Tell me how long you were in Krasnik.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I-- I married the 1st of September. And then right away came the High Holidays, which I had to conduct the services. That's why I got married on the night of the first [NON-ENGLISH]. In 1945, it was Saturday night, the 1st of September, the first [NON-ENGLISH]. And after our marriage, I had to go and say the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I don't remember whether it was on Rosh Hashanah or Sukkot, I got myself a little room where we lived, and I stood up to chant the kiddush. A stone fell through-- was thrown into the [NON-ENGLISH], they call them, in the front of the windows. You know, they had those shutters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought like a bomb had been. And we-- I opened up the door. And some Polacks through, they heard chanting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something in Hebrew. They threw a big stone in then. And they split the window there. And then my wife said, we can't stay in here. And it was I-- maybe about four weeks after we got married. And she said, we can't stay in here. We have to move on. This is not the life for us. And then she said, you know, you also may be taken to the army, to the Polish army. I was at that age, that I was 24 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, let's go. I said, where do we go? Let's go more to the west, where we could maybe find a way where to immigrate to Palestine, at that time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went on the train, and someone told us that there is some acquaintances from us in a town, Gliwice. There are a lot of empty apartments, and you could find something there. So we went to Gleiwitz. We came in there, and near the acquaintances, we found a little apartment, a room with, with, with a little kitchenette. And we stood there, and from there, we started to go out every day to find out where we could get hooked up to what kind of group to emigrate, to go out, to leave Poland. At that time, I understand it was the Berihaha called from Palestine. They came young men, and they organized to get out as many Jews from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got on that group. And they told us, on a certain day, about a few weeks later, we have to go out and come to a train, and we will be able-- we will have to walk from a certain place to a station, and then go. They will-- we will appoint them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing, don't have any documents with yourself. Don't speak Polish, because if you're going to speak Polish, they'll recognize that you're Jewish. You may [INAUDIBLE] in trouble. So what could we do? They said, we go as, as a Greek group-- Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --And that we go back to Saloniki-- Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: Salonika.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all he should remember is Salonika. And if they approach you and they speak to you, tell them something from the siddur.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did. And of course, the Polish police went around us. We already are ready to go on the train, to take a ride into-- to border-- cross the border. They speak. They speak. If they come over, they say, [NON-ENGLISH] in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I told them is [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And they said something-- another one said [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Another one would say something else in Hebrew. And they got so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mixed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: --mixed up, they said, [SPEAKING POLISH]. That means the Greeks are worse than the Jews. They don't understand anything. And we got on the train. And we are ready to leave to Czechoslovakia. While we sit on the train, a big officer-- I don't know, a [NON-ENGLISH], a Russian officer, with-- you know-- high-rank officer, like a captain or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or a colonel or major.

speaker: interviewee
text: A colonel. He comes in, and he says [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means, people, where are you going? He looks at me, just like at me. So I told him, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], [SPEAKING HEBREW]. He finished the sentence for me in Hebrew, and I felt so good. And I figured that he is one of ours. And he must be, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would know?

speaker: interviewee
text: None other. Who, who would know to finish the sentence? And he had a big smile on his face, and he let us go. And we went in to-- drove into Czechoslovakia. And I think it was we came in to Bratislava. In Bratislava, we were maybe a day, and they told us that we-- tonight, we're going to go out. And we have to go to walk over Alps--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --There, the Alps into-- into Austria, to go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't remember exactly how, but we got to a place, and it was midnight. It started. There was a transport of maybe 100 people. And some are carrying little children too that they were redeemed from homes that were left during the war. And my brother and I-- and by that time, my wife said, I think I'm pregnant. And we are going, and the-- soon my brother heard that she's pregnant, it was wild. He said, well don't let her carry anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in the second month, it-- that's dangerous. And he had a wife, but he didn't have any children, but he was so anxious not to let her carry anything. So we had little things to carry. And we go out, and it was such a rainy, freezing day, our clothes froze on us. The clothes on us was like, like, like ice. And we came to a place. And we-- that was that we crossed the Alpines, the Alps.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Austrian Alps.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Austrian Alps. And then we came to a place where each one of us was given a ticket. And we walked for about three blocks to a station. And we got on a train, on a more modern train. And from there, they took us to Vienna. The conductors came in. They checked. Everyone gave their ticket. We didn't know how we got the ticket, where it was we came into.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through Berihah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through, through the Berihah. It was an underground-- it was done everything for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, to this day, I'm very-- we are very appreciative about this. When we came in, they took us to Rothschild-- it used to be the Rothschild--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rothschild.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Rothschild Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in [INAUDIBLE] whatever it was, they took us there. And from-- it wasn't anymore the hospital there. And they took-- they devote-- it was like a-- like a place where they gather people from all over. We didn't have any beds, normal beds to sleep, but they were-- they had bunks there, too. But it was a little bit better. And we stood there for about maybe a month, maybe not that much. I don't remember. I remember we were hungry. We went out. We, we tried to organize a few coupons.

speaker: interviewee
text: We bought some bread, a little extra piece herring. And we lived it through. Then we met, in Vienna, some people who told us, what are you staying in this camp? You know it, you need another DP camp. It's a DP-- Displaced Person-- camp. Come to Amberg in Germany. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we will see that you get where to live. And you'll live near us. And they were from my town. They were left three brothers. One got blind in the camp. Their name was [? Edelmann. ?] And they all still alive today. And we went to Amberg. And we reached Amberg, must have been about three, four days before Christmas, because I remember it was Hanukkah time at that time. And right away, we went to the town hall, and we told them, we are DPs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave us a room, a, a beautiful room. And it was kitchen privileges out from the hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived there. It wasn't a DP camp, but we received some help from the owner, owner there. And we lived there in Amberg--

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived there for a number of years.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, now I'm going to tell you the story what happened to my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years after the war was finished, my brother didn't remarry. And he lived in another apartment, but in Amberg. And he presumably got into an argument with some detectives or police. And they had a struggle of some kind. And on the 25th of May-- of May, he was shot to death. At the same time, while he was shot, it was Erev Shavuot. It came out just like this, on Shabbos before Shavuot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know what happened to him, but they arrested me, too. And then they let me go. I said, what was-- what, what-- why did you arrest me? What did I do? Well, your brother had a struggle, and he was shot. Oh, when I heard this came out, believe me. I remember the Kultusminister, Auerbach, was at that time Jewish in the German government. And he came down to Amberg. And the, the detective that shot him produced a, a, a certificate that he had-- he-- that he argued with him, and he almost choked him. He had signs on his back, and he called him Hitlerist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Auerbach said, this why you did it, in self-defense, right? But why did you have to put in five bullets in him? He said, I don't remember how many. You know, of course, you didn't have any witnesses to it. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know that we buried him in Amberg. And to his funeral, if there have been not 100,000 people in this funeral, because it was advertised all over in the papers that a Jew was shot by German police because of what-- they, they came up with the whole thing. And all the Jews that were in Munchen and in Landsberg, and-- they heard that-- and especially the [INAUDIBLE] men from Krasnik-- they all came to the funeral. The funeral took place-- today is Wednesday, the second day of Shavuot. And I remember that for six miles to the cemetery, we walked. I mean, the, the, the, the body was on a-- on a horse and carriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all, all, all others-- even, even the Germans-- walked. The Social Democrats

speaker: interviewer
text: Social Democrats, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --went along with this, because at that time, it was a shock to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, after the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the story of my-- and my daughter happened to be just a year old, because she was born on the 6th of June, and it was the second day of Shavuot a year before.

speaker: interviewer
text: The following year. A year--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he arranged to bring yet a cake to have a birthday party for her. But he never returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You finally came to this country. Tell me about what, what you did when you came to this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to this country, and we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You came here '49--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we found-- no, let me tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, I knew I had an uncle somewhere, but I didn't remember the name exactly. I remembered [? Hyman, ?] and I said either [? Schuhmann ?] or [? Schuhberg. ?] I couldn't remember. And I had a Jewish soldier who speak-- spoke Yiddish. And he said to me, would you remember any address? And I said, I think it's 320 Sea Breeze, Breeze--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sea Breeze.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Sea Breeze.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Brighton Beach.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brighton Beach.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brooklyn, I said. He said, don't worry. I have a mother who does nothing but finds people, relatives, in Brooklyn. And he gave my-- took my address. It didn't take a few weeks, and I got a letter from my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my God.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he notified to me through another uncle, a cousin of my father. His name was Mordechai Wagner, Mordechai Wagner. And when this Mordechai Wagner heard that I was left alive, because we were doubly-- you know, he was a cousin to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also his sister was married to an uncle of mine. So we were double--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: --close. And of course, I didn't know him, because he left the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: --maybe when I was a baby. So did my uncle. Now, my uncle wasn't a real uncle. He was a step-uncle. But I'll tell you, he and my mother were sister and brother of one mother, but not the same father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was anxious to hear what happened to family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he made provisions to send us an affidavit. We should come-- of course, this didn't take so fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meanwhile, while we were still to go to Israel, we had already passports to go to Palestine. A friend of mine who was with us in camp brought us a letter from Cyprus. He said, if you want, with your baby to, to enter another DP, don't go now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Israel was not yet established.

speaker: interviewee
text: They closed the doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were closed. And any boat that came illegal, they took them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Went to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: --into Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember that, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were waiting, and then this opportunity availed themselves. And we left to Bremen. And we went with General [INAUDIBLE] boat, ship, to the United States. And believe me, for traveling for 10 days, and if I didn't say at one time during that ship that I should have been staying where I-- who needs this? We were so sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't continue. But you know, that, that, that, the, the will to live and go on to life-- finally we got used to this sickness of the sea, too. And my wife was sick. I was sick. I didn't see my wife for six, seven days. I couldn't. I was-- I was laid away. And my daughter, she was, at that time, already three years old. They took her in on the ship in, in this-- the hospital, in the lazarette, in the hospital. And we used-- we-- my wife used to climb up there and to go in and see where, where the child of ours, that precious child of ours, is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They threw her out. And she said in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Why do they chase you out? She couldn't understand. When we arrived in Brooklyn, this uncle, Wagner, not my uncle [? Schuhmann, ?] was waiting for us with a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, God.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took us to the home of my uncle [? Schuhmann. ?] And we started to live with him. The life wasn't so rosy. We-- they were busy people. They couldn't understand us very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And pretty soon, I started to go to work. And after, after 10 days, I said, I've got to do something. What shall I do? Of course, I knew all my life that after I was liberated from camp through my voice, that my life was-- Singing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Spared. So I said I would like to be a cantor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you become a cantor?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. In Germany, while I was live, I trained with a, a, a private tutor. I used to go to Nuremberg and train my voice. And then it came into not everybody could conduct services. So while I was in Germany, they took me one time to Pegnitz to conduct services. Another year, for Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, I went to Tirschenreuth, which was not far from Bayreuth or whatever it's called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beirut.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's called Bayreuth, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bayreuth, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever it is. And in the city, in Amberg, where I lived, I used to daven on Shabbat and used to read the Torah. And I got a little bit paid for it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] That helps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And came to the United States, I couldn't speak English. And I said to myself, I'm a man of 26 years old-- what was I, 28 already, years old. I felt a shame. I didn't know how to say hello in English. But I started to go to high school-- to night school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Night school. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Night school, I started to take up. My wife went with me, too. And, and we struggled. And I remember I went in to buy a paper, I said, give me a pepper.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave me a pepper. And I-- And then I took-- no, no, I said a news-pepper. Oh. They gave me a newspaper. And they said, how much-- and I said, how much is it? And they said, a nickel. And I didn't know what a nickel at that time was. If they would told me five--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Cent, I would know. Or a dime. Who knew a dime? $0.10 I knew. I mean, could-- this is a story in itself. You know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that I wanted to get rid of, of the-- my aunt and uncle's apartment, to live with them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you wanted to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I want to get out. So they told me, go, go to Brighton Beach, to Coney Island. You find in the end of September-- no, after Labor Day, there are apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went one day, and I got home and my aunt said, hey, did you find anything? I said, I don't have any luck. Wherever I come, it says, apartment too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rooms too late. It was to let, and I read too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came home, and I said, well. They said, no, it's not too late. It's to let. Oh, finally I found an apartment in Brighton Beach, in a small room downstairs. And slowly from this room, we got to an apartment in-- on Stockton Street in, in-- what was it called? East New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think they still call that Williamsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Williamsburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Williamsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to go to work as a-- at a tailor shop. Then they sent me out to examine to New Jersey. So-- But still, it wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't what you wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't what, what, what I wanted. And I remember, I had the [INAUDIBLE] Erev Shavuos living on Stockton Street, which was before they came out also like Shabbos before, and they gave me maftir. When I said the maftir, they asked me if I would daven Musaf. And I davened Musaf for them. And that summer-- that summer-- of course, before, I got-- I, I got the two shteles in 1951. In 1950, I had two shteles. In 1951, I davened at the Fallsberg--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the Pines.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Pines Hotel. Right. And then for Yom Kippur, they weren't open. So I was hired to daven in the-- in Flatbush, in a congregation [INAUDIBLE], which means for the martyrs, the shul for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- I didn't even get paid for it. But it was an honor for me to be able to daven in a shul with this rabbi who was also a Holocaust survivor. I davened Yom Kippur. But on that July before High Holidays, I got a call in the morning. And they said, I came from Washington, and we need a chazzan, a cantor for High Holidays. Would you accept a shtele?

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, who told you about it? He said, my mother goes to the shul, and she lives a block away from you. And she heard you in that shul on Stockton Street, and she told me that you are good chazzan. Therefore, I want to come over and talk to you. So he came over-- by the name of Scotty Feldman-- and he came over, and he engaged me right away for High Holidays to go to Maryland, College Park, in Washington. I got paid $800 for a job which I never thought was a lot of money. It's not a lot of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, for me, was a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know what I did? I came home after Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, with that much money. I said to [INAUDIBLE], I've got to-- have to know how to-- I have to learn English. I must learn how to read English. She said, how could you? It takes time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out and bought for $600 a DuMont television. I said, I hear them talking, and I'll learn. But you know what, it was the wrong thing for me to do. That DuMont television, all I watched is cowboys. I couldn't understand anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's funny. It looks funny, but that was the purpose. And my wife said--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are a [NON-ENGLISH] You buy a television, we don't have anything to go on with. That's it. But that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here for a minute. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Five more minutes? Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, and then I assumed a permanent position for cantor to be in Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Washington, DC. And I was there till 1956. And I wasn't used to daven in a conservative shul. As I told you, it was French to me. And I davened like I davened at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the rabbi said to me, you know, you belong to a-- to a--

speaker: interviewer
text: An orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: --A more Orthodox shul. Because this is not for you. I couldn't stand the English reading and the responsive reading. Of course, now I'm used to it, and I participate in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, it was strange to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was new, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got myself a new shtele.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a new position. And I joined the Cantors Assembly.

speaker: interviewer
text: United Synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: The United Synagogue and Cantors Assembly in 1955. And in 1956, I got a shtele in Williamsport, Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you're still in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Williamsport, Pennsylvania. And I enjoyed there for few years. And then-- and then I said, I've got to go back to Washington, because my daughter got already 15 years old, and I didn't want that she started to make, you know, homework with non-Jewish students. I was afraid thus. Also I got back to Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there in Washington for seven years. Until--

speaker: interviewer
text: Until?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the rabbi came up. They wanted to use women for the minyan and give them full participation. And I'm used to it today, but at that time, I wasn't used to it. That's [NON-ENGLISH]. And I just-- then I, I notified the congregation. And I quit the congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Takes [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Cantors Assembly told me, we applaud you for it, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the trend.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the newest style. They said, the trend. You have to get used to it. And then I didn't have a new shtele. I said, I don't want a new position. It's, it's-- I couldn't take it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I get a call from the-- from the conservative movement, and from, from the Cantors Assembly, who was-- he was an officer with the-- what do you call this? The United Synagogue. And he calls me up, and I knew him. And he said, Morris, would you like to go to Canada for cantors for-- A shtele.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for a shtele? I said, well, give me another call. I mean, I don't know. Not for a shtele, but just for Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Oh, just Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hashanah and Kippur. I said, you see, my wife isn't here. When she'll come back, I'll discuss with her, and I'll call her back. And he gives me all the information. He said, you have time to call me till about 7:30 in the evening. And I'll be in this and this address. My wife came home. She said-- She said to me, you don't want to be a chazzan anymore. I said, you're right. But a few minutes later, I said, maybe I should go in and find out. She said, you said you don't want. We made arrangement to go with the [INAUDIBLE] and blah, blah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I wait another 20 minutes, and I said, you know what? A cantor is like an etrog and a lulav. He's good till Erev Sukkot. If you don't sell the etrog and the lulav, Erev Sukkot, you could throw him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said-- she said, no, I see you want to go. Go in. So I called up the, the cantor, my colleague, and I said to him, when do you want me to come in? He said, when could you come in? I said I could take the latest plane, 6 o'clock in the evening. He said, good. I'll have some people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Waiting for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --waiting for you. I come in and there was a whole group sitting. I said, what are you doing in here? Said we were interviewed. I said, he told me I'm the only one. I wouldn't have come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my God.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. So I said, what are you doing? He said, one says he's a, a salesman. The other one said he is a clerk in a-- in the bank. The other one has a grocery store. I said, they're not hazzanim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just wanted the position for-- I came in, and there was the rabbi from Calgary. And I didn't know where Calgary is. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the west coast.

speaker: interviewee
text: The west coast. Finally, from all the people, I got the shtele. And I came to Calgary. And I was there for 16 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
